text: The surgeon examines the transverse colon, identifying the tattoo marks placed proximal and distal to the area initially described as being in the cecum.
The surgeon examines the transverse colon, identifying a tattoo mark that was intended to be placed in the cecum, with additional tattoos visible proximal and distal to the initial site.

The surgeon has docked the robotic system and begins the extended right hemicolectomy by isolating the ileocolic vessels at the junction with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The vein is divided first, followed by the artery, with medial dissection performed to separate the tissue.

The surgeon has docked the robotic system and begins the extended right hemicolectomy by isolating the ileocolic vessels at their junction with the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The vein is divided first, followed by the artery, with medial dissection performed to separate the tissue.

The surgeon navigates up the superior mesenteric vessels towards the middle colic arteries, identifying the right branch of the middle colic. The mesenteric planned transection is marked to maintain orientation, with visible progress across the middle colic area and marking of the distal margin.

The surgeon navigates up the superior mesenteric vessels towards the middle colic vessels, identifying the right branch of the middle colic. The mesenteric planned transection is marked to maintain orientation, with the surgeon visually marking the distal margin across the middle colic area.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and proceeds with the mesenteric transection, isolating and dividing the right colic artery followed by the right colic vein. The surgeon then targets the middle colic pedicle for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and continues the mesenteric transection, isolating and dividing the right colic artery followed by the right colic vein. The surgeon then proceeds to address the middle colic pedicle.
The surgeon isolates and takes the middle colic artery, which is visible in the surgical field, followed by the dissection and division of the middle colic vein.
The surgeon isolates and divides the middle colic artery, which comes into view, followed by the dissection and division of the middle colic vein.
The surgeon proceeds from the planned distal margin at the bowel wall, dissecting down to the mesenteric area, carefully transecting the intervening mesentery and marginal branches.
The surgeon approaches the planned distal margin at the bowel wall, dissecting down to the mesentery and transecting the intervening mesentery along with the marginal branches.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, followed by lateral mobilization. The gastrocolic ligament is taken down, and dissection proceeds around the hepatic flexure, moving down the lateral attachments to the right colon and cecum.

The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the tumor, followed by lateral mobilization. The gastrocolic ligament is taken down, and dissection proceeds around the hepatic flexure. The lateral attachments to the right colon and cecum are separated, with the surgeon adopting an inferior approach to complete the lateral attachments of the specimen.
The surgeon approaches the specimen from an inferior angle to complete the dissection of the lateral attachments, ensuring the specimen is fully mobilized.

The surgeon transects the small bowel mesentery up to the planned proximal margin, checking perfusion. The redundant transverse colon allows for stapling through the SuperP report despite the tumor's unusual location.
The surgeon takes the small bowel mesentery to the planned proximal margin, preparing to check the perfusion of the tissue.

The surgeon staples the small bowel and positions the specimen down to the pelvis for later extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision. An isoperistaltic anastomosis is lined up, with stay sutures placed at the far and near corners. A colotomy and enterotomy are performed, followed by stapling from the suprapubic port, which is the only incision larger than twelve millimeters.

The surgeon staples the small bowel and places the specimen into the pelvis for later extraction through a Pfannenstiel incision. An isoperistaltic anastomosis is lined up, with stay sutures already placed at the far and near corners. A colotomy and enterotomy are made, followed by stapling from the suprapubic port, which is the only incision larger than twelve millimeters.
The surgeon sews closed the common channel, beginning at the near corner and proceeding around the channel. A transition stitch is used, followed by a baseball stitch up to the far corner.
The surgeon sews closed the common channel, beginning at the near corner and proceeding around the channel. A transition stitch is used, followed by a baseball stitch technique up to the far corner.
The surgeon imbricates the tissue, folding and suturing it back down to secure the area after previous mobilization and dissection steps.
The surgeon imbricates the tissue, folding and securing it back down to reinforce the surgical site.
